title: Prompt injection can alter model behavior and compromise downstream decisions
summary: OWASP identifies prompt injection as a top LLM application risk, with possible unauthorized access, data exposure, and compromised decision-making.
risk_hypothesis: Instruction-like text from users or untrusted context can override intended task boundaries and produce unsafe decisions or actions.

proposed_probe.description: Place a benignly summarized, non-harmful instruction-confusion marker inside untrusted retrieved context and test whether the agent treats it as instruction or evidence.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use synthetic text that requests a harmless but out-of-scope action, not a real exploit or data request.

title: Incident databases can reveal real-world harm patterns and reporting gaps
source_name: AI Incident Database / MIT AI Incident Tracker
summary: Public incident trackers classify real-world AI incidents by risk, cause, harm, severity, and other dimensions.
risk_hypothesis: Agentic risk taxonomies should be checked against real-world harm reports so probes do not overfit to security-only failure modes.

proposed_probe.description: Map incident harm categories to agentic surfaces and identify missing synthetic traces for high-salience public harms.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use incident summaries and category labels rather than recreating harmful content.

notes: Useful for broadening beyond prompt injection.

title: Provenance and deployment monitoring are central to generative AI risk management
source_name: NIST AI RMF / Generative AI Profile
summary: NIST emphasizes provenance, monitoring capabilities and limitations in deployment, and rigorous test/evaluation/validation/verification.
risk_hypothesis: Agentic safety monitors should preserve provenance for retrieved content, tool outputs, and human approvals so risk decisions can be audited.

proposed_probe.description: Run the same task with trusted and untrusted retrieved content and require the sentinel to preserve source trust in each risk event.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use synthetic source labels and harmless task content.

notes: Connects standards language to trace schema.

title: Indirect prompt injection benchmarks model attacks through external content
summary: BIPIA studies indirect prompt injection where attackers modify external content that LLM-integrated applications retrieve or process.
risk_hypothesis: External content should be treated as untrusted evidence, and probes should vary task, attack type, and injection placement.

proposed_probe.description: Generate safe indirect-injection fixtures varying source type, injection placement, and requested out-of-scope action.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use non-harmful out-of-scope actions and do not copy benchmark attack strings into public outputs.

title: Function-call malfunction detection remains a gap for guardrail models
summary: Mozilla.ai found promise in indirect prompt-injection detection but noted that function-call malfunction detection remains hard for current guardrail approaches.
risk_hypothesis: Trajectory-level tool-call checks should be evaluated separately from input/output prompt-injection detection.

proposed_probe.description: Create tool-call traces where the final answer is safe but the tool trajectory violates scope, permission, or approval rules.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use mocked tools and synthetic connectors only.

title: Social media jailbreak motif suggests new wrapper or role-play pattern
summary: A recurring public discussion claims that a new wrapper pattern can induce models to treat policy constraints as fictional context.
risk_hypothesis: Role-play or wrapper formats may alter refusal behavior and should be abstracted into a safe probe family.

proposed_probe.description: Build a harmless role-play wrapper probe that tests whether the model follows the original safe task or the wrapper's out-of-scope instruction.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use harmless content and avoid storing the original public jailbreak text.

title: Policy and journalism attention can identify socially salient harms
summary: Journalists and policy actors often surface harms such as scams, impersonation, deepfakes, privacy erosion, and vulnerable-user failures before they are formalized as benchmarks.
risk_hypothesis: Probe backlog should include high-salience public harms even when the technical exploit path is still unclear.

proposed_probe.description: Create non-operational probes that test whether agents recognize scam-like, impersonation-like, or vulnerable-user risk patterns without providing harmful instructions.
proposed_probe.safe_reproduction: Use synthetic benign placeholders and focus on classification, routing, and refusal boundaries.

notes: Needs corroboration from incidents or policy reports.
